title: Evaluating Classification Quality
concept: Calculating F1-score, precision, and recall on a holdout set of user reviews.

exercises.prompt: What is the goal of backpropagation in training a sentiment analyzer?

options.text: To increase loss and improve accuracy.
To decrease loss and improve accuracy.
To split data into training and testing sets.

exercises.explain: Backpropagation aims to decrease loss, which means improving the model's accuracy on the training data.

cards.title: Why Evaluate Quality?
cards.body: Evaluating quality helps us understand how well our model classifies sentiments from user reviews. It's like checking if your recipe turned out right by tasting the food!

cards.title: What is F1-Score?
cards.body: The F1-score is a measure that combines both precision and recall. It is used to evaluate the balance between correctly identifying positive sentiments and minimizing false positives.

cards.title: Balancing Act
cards.body: Imagine a seesaw. On one side, you have precision (correctly guessed positives) and on the other, recall (capturing all true positives). The F1-score helps keep this seesaw balanced!

cards.title: Example: User Reviews
cards.body: If we classify 80 reviews as positive but only 60 were actually positive, precision is 60/80. If there are 100 positive reviews in total, recall is 60/100.

cards.title: Myth: F1-Score is All You Need
cards.body: Just looking at the F1-score can be misleading! Always consider precision and recall together for a full picture.

cards.title: Visual Comparison
cards.body: Precision and recall are key parts to understand the F1-score. Let's visualize them!

left.text: Percentage of positive predictions that are correct.

right.text: Percentage of actual positives that we predicted.

exercises.prompt: Which metric combines precision and recall to provide a single score?
exercises.explain: The F1-score combines both precision and recall, providing a balanced measure of a model's performance.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Recall is the number of true positives out of total positive predictions.
exercises.explain: This statement is false because recall measures the number of true positives out of all actual positive cases.

exercises.prompt: Precision is calculated by taking the number of true positives and dividing it by the total number of _____ predictions.
exercises.sentence: Precision is calculated by taking the number of true positives and dividing it by the total number of positive predictions.
exercises.explain: Precision focuses on the accuracy of positive predictions, so it's critical to use 'positive predictions'.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is NOT an important metric in evaluating your sentiment analyzer?
exercises.explain: Null accuracy measures the most frequent class and isn't helpful for understanding model performance.

exercises.prompt: If you have a precision of 70%, a recall of 80%, what is the F1-score approximately?
exercises.explain: The F1-score can be calculated using the formula 2 * (Precision * Recall) / (Precision + Recall). Here, it balances precision and recall, leading to an approximate score of 75%. Keep practicing to master evaluating models!
